title: Data Warehouse / ETL Ingestion — System Design
description: Design the data platform that ingests data from many source systems and turns it into a queryable analytical warehouse. The themes are batch + CDC ingestion into a raw lake, ELT transformation into modeled tables, schema evolution, partitioning for query performance, and reliable backfills.

nodes.label: Source Systems
nodes.description: Operational DBs, event streams, third-party APIs feeding the warehouse.

nodes.label: CDC / Connectors
nodes.description: Captures DB changes and pulls API/batch extracts into the lake.

nodes.label: Event Stream
nodes.description: Real-time events ingested for streaming/CDC paths.

nodes.label: Landing / Raw Lake
nodes.description: Immutable raw extracts as ingested (the replay source of truth).

nodes.label: Load Service
nodes.description: Loads raw data into staging tables; idempotent per batch/offset.

nodes.label: Transformation Engine (ELT)
nodes.description: SQL/Spark transforms staging into modeled marts.

nodes.label: Warehouse (Columnar)
nodes.description: Modeled, partitioned analytical tables for queries.

nodes.label: Pipeline Orchestrator
nodes.description: Schedules/DAGs the load + transform jobs; handles backfills.

nodes.label: Data Quality / Monitoring
nodes.description: Validates freshness, row counts, schema, and anomalies.

nodes.label: Governance / Access Control
nodes.description: Classifies sensitive columns, enforces least-privilege access + masking, audits reads, and applies retention/deletion/legal-hold policy over the raw lake and warehouse.

functional: Ingest from many sources: operational DBs (CDC), event streams, batch/API extracts.
Land raw data immutably, then transform it into clean, modeled analytical tables.
Serve analytical queries / BI dashboards over the modeled data.
Handle schema changes in sources without breaking the pipeline.
Backfill / reprocess historical data when logic or schemas change.
nonFunctional: Reliable, idempotent loads — re-running a load produces the same result (no dupes).
Reprocessable: raw data is retained so any output can be rebuilt.
Query performance over large tables (partitioning, columnar).
Freshness appropriate per dataset (batch hourly/daily; CDC near-real-time).
Scale to many sources and TBs/day; correctness/auditability of the data.
Governed raw retention: raw is encrypted, access is least-privilege and audited, sensitive columns are classified/masked, and retention/deletion (incl. legal hold) is enforced.

capacity.label: Sources
capacity.note: DBs, streams, APIs — different shapes/cadences. Connector parallelism scales to the 50 hot tables.

capacity.label: Raw ingest
capacity.note: Drives object-storage throughput + small-file/compaction pressure in the lake.

capacity.label: Stored volume
capacity.note: Raw + staging + modeled + snapshots + compaction overhead. 5 TB/day raw -> ~10-20 TB/day landed across layers.

capacity.label: Retention
capacity.note: 90 days hot/queryable modeled data; raw kept 1-7 years (policy) to enable reprocessing/backfill.

capacity.label: Freshness
capacity.note: Per-dataset SLA; sizes transform cluster and orchestrator cadence.

capacity.label: Query load
capacity.note: Plus ~20 long-running dashboard queries — needs warehouse concurrency controls / queue isolation.

capacity.label: Backfill
capacity.note: Bounded parallel partitions, admission-controlled below fresh-data priority so SLAs hold.

capacity.label: Load semantics
capacity.note: Re-running a partition load is safe (overwrite, not append).

api.description: Trigger (or schedule) a pipeline run over a partition/range: load, transform to a temp snapshot, validate, then atomically publish. The idempotency key makes a re-trigger re-publish the same partition rather than duplicate it.

messages.label: discard temp; last good snapshot stays; state=failed, alert owner

api.description: Minimal synchronous query over modeled warehouse tables (small results). For governed, queued, cancellable work with cost attribution, use the async query-job contract below.

Submit an analytical query as a governed job. The platform resolves the caller's effective access (deny / allow / column-masked) before admitting it to a workload queue, estimates bytes to scan against the budget policy, and returns a job id to poll. This is what makes workload isolation, quotas, and cost attribution from step 5 a real contract rather than prose.

messages.label: estimate bytes; admit to workload queue

api.description: Poll a query job: lifecycle state, queue/warehouse it ran on, bytes scanned vs estimate, compute seconds, and cost attribution back to the principal/budget. State walks queued -> running -> succeeded | failed | cancelled.
Cancel a running or queued query job so a runaway scan can't exhaust a queue's concurrency or blow the budget. Returns the bytes scanned so far for cost attribution.

Get run status, attempt, watermark, published snapshot, and quality results. State walks queued -> loading -> transforming -> validating -> publishing -> succeeded | failed | cancelled (or blocked while waiting on a dependency). The terminal payload also carries failureReason/blockedBy, cancelRequestedAt, and completedAt; only the publishing step makes output visible (a committed snapshot).

Request cancel of an in-flight run. A cancel only takes effect before the atomic publish: stamping cancelRequestedAt aborts the unpublished temp snapshot (state=aborted) and the last good committed snapshot stays visible. Once publishing has committed, the run is already succeeded and there is nothing to cancel.
Retry a failed run idempotently (same idempotency key re-publishes the partition, no duplicates). The retry re-enters the lifecycle at loading.
Register a new schema version for a table; the registry validates the compatibility mode.
List quality-check results gating a partition's publish (blocking vs advisory).
Trace upstream/downstream lineage for a dataset (sources, transform versions, outputs).
Inspect a dataset's governance posture: column classifications, masking strategy, retention/legal-hold, and effective access for the caller.
Request deletion of a subject/key across raw + modeled layers; rejected while a legal hold is in force, otherwise crypto-shreds and records an audit event.
Reprocess a historical date range from raw data, isolated from fresh-data SLAs.

dataModel.name: raw_extract (landing)
dataModel.note: Immutable raw data as ingested, partitioned by ingest time/source; carries the CDC contract.

fields.name: offset / lsn
fields.type: connector checkpoint, for replay + idempotent reload

dataModel.name: warehouse_table (modeled)
dataModel.note: Clean, typed, partitioned analytical table (star-schema fact/dim).

dataModel.name: source
dataModel.note: A registered upstream system/table the platform ingests from.

dataModel.name: pipeline
dataModel.note: A DAG definition that loads + transforms one or more datasets.

dataModel.name: pipeline_run / task_run
dataModel.note: One execution of a pipeline (and its tasks): the unit of idempotency + lineage.

fields.name: state
fields.type: enum(queued, loading, transforming, validating, publishing, succeeded, failed, cancelled, blocked)

fields.name: failure_reason / blocked_by
fields.type: why it failed; run/dep ids it waits on

dataModel.name: output_snapshot
dataModel.note: An atomically-published version of a partition/table; queries pin to committed snapshots.

dataModel.name: schema_version
dataModel.note: Versioned schema for a source/table, with compatibility + mapping rules.

dataModel.name: quality_check_result
dataModel.note: Outcome of a validation gate for a run/snapshot; can block or advise.

dataModel.name: lineage_edge
dataModel.note: Provenance edge: which input + transform produced which output.

dataModel.name: backfill_job
dataModel.note: A controlled historical reprocess, isolated from fresh-data SLAs.

fields.name: priority / concurrency_cap
fields.type: below fresh data; max parallel partitions

dataModel.name: query_job
dataModel.note: A governed analytical query: admitted to a workload queue, access-resolved, and cost-attributed.

fields.name: compute_seconds / cost_attribution
fields.type: compute used; charged to principal/budget policy

dataModel.name: data_classification
dataModel.note: Tags a column/dataset with a sensitivity class that drives masking + access rules.

dataModel.name: access_policy
dataModel.note: Least-privilege grant: which principal may read which classified data, and how.

fields.name: dataset / class_scope
fields.type: what they can reach; max sensitivity

dataModel.name: retention_policy / legal_hold
dataModel.note: How long raw/modeled data lives and when it must NOT be deleted.

dataModel.name: deletion_request
dataModel.note: A per-subject erasure (e.g. GDPR) tracked across raw + modeled layers; blocked while a legal hold is in force.

fields.name: request_id / subject_key
fields.type: uuid + the key being erased (e.g. user:42)

fields.name: blocked_by_legal_hold
fields.type: bool — pauses erasure while a hold is set

dataModel.name: audit_event
dataModel.note: Immutable record of who read/changed sensitive data, for compliance + investigation.

patterns.name: ELT (load raw, transform in-warehouse)
patterns.what: Land raw data first, then transform inside the warehouse, instead of transforming before load (ETL).
patterns.whenToUse: Modern cloud warehouses; keeps raw for reprocessing and uses warehouse compute.
patterns.group: Ingest into a Raw Lake (ELT, not ETL)

patterns.name: CDC + batch ingestion
patterns.what: Capture DB changes (CDC) and pull batch/API extracts, landing both immutably.
patterns.group: Ingest into a Raw Lake (ELT, not ETL)

patterns.name: Idempotent partitioned loads
patterns.what: Load by partition with idempotency so re-runs replace rather than duplicate.

patterns.name: Atomic snapshot publish
patterns.what: Write a temp/unpublished snapshot, validate it, then commit it atomically; only committed snapshots are queryable and only then does the watermark advance.
patterns.whenToUse: Any time readers must never see a half-written partition and failed runs must leave the last good data in place.

patterns.name: Governed raw lake
patterns.what: Encrypt raw, classify sensitive columns on landing, enforce least-privilege + audited access, mask/tokenize modeled outputs per class, and propagate retention/deletion/legal-hold across raw and modeled.
patterns.whenToUse: ELT platforms that retain unmodified raw, which concentrates sensitivity and erasure risk in the lake.
patterns.group: Ingest into a Raw Lake (ELT, not ETL)

patterns.name: Schema registry + evolution
patterns.what: Track table schemas/versions and evolve them safely (additive, typed).
patterns.whenToUse: Sources whose schemas change over time.

patterns.name: Partitioning + columnar
patterns.what: Partition tables (by date) and store columnar so queries prune and scan less.

patterns.name: Workload isolation + cost controls
patterns.what: Run dashboards and ad-hoc analysts on separate warehouses/queues with concurrency limits, quotas/budgets, kill/cancel, and per-query cost attribution.
patterns.whenToUse: Mixed interactive + heavy workloads where one scan could starve others or blow the budget.

patterns.name: Reprocessing / backfill
patterns.what: Rebuild outputs from retained raw data when logic/schema changes.
patterns.whenToUse: Fixing bugs or applying new transforms to history.

patterns.name: Quality-gated orchestration
patterns.what: Run DAG nodes only when dependencies are fresh, transform to a temp snapshot, then let blocking quality checks gate the atomic publish (advisory checks warn but allow).
patterns.whenToUse: Multi-pipeline platforms where a bad partition must never become queryable or propagate to dependents.

patterns.name: Run/snapshot lineage
patterns.what: Record, per run, which source offsets and transform version produced which output snapshot — lineage at run/snapshot granularity, not just table-to-table edges.
patterns.whenToUse: Debugging, impact analysis, and audit across an evolving pipeline.

steps.title: 1. Naive Baseline: One Script Transforms Then Loads Into the Operational DB
description: Start with the simplest thing that works. A nightly cron script reads from the source systems, transforms the rows in memory (joins, type casts, business logic), and loads the result straight into one operational database that analysts query directly. Classic transform-then-load ETL into a single DB.

This is correct for a small shop and exposes two problems the rest of the design exists to solve. First, the transform throws away the raw data — once a transform has a bug or the business logic changes, there is nothing to reprocess from; you can only re-pull whatever the source still has. Second, pointing analysts' heavy scans at the same database the application writes to means analytical queries contend with operational load and don't scale to TBs.

So the baseline motivates the whole design: keep the raw data (load it first, transform later — ELT), and serve analytics from a separate, partitioned columnar warehouse instead of the operational DB.

view.caption: A nightly transformation engine reads the source systems and writes straight into the single operational database that analysts query directly — no separate analytical store yet.

steps.decisionPrompt: If your script transforms rows and then loads them, where do you go to fix three months of data when you find the transform had a bug?
whyNow: Establishing the transform-then-load baseline first makes it obvious why raw data must be retained (ELT) and why analytics needs its own store — every later step removes a specific pain from here.

recap.after: A script transforms source rows and loads them into one operational DB analysts query directly.
recap.newRisk: Transformed-away raw data can't be reprocessed, and analytical scans on the operational DB don't scale.

traps.trap: Shipping transform-then-load into the operational DB as 'good enough'.
traps.why: Discarding raw data makes bug fixes and logic changes unrecoverable, and analytics queries contend with live application traffic.
traps.instead: Load raw first (ELT) into an immutable lake, then transform into a separate partitioned columnar warehouse.

steps.title: 2. Ingest into a Raw Lake (ELT, not ETL)
description: Pull from heterogeneous sources: change-data-capture (CDC) from operational DBs, event streams, and batch/API extracts. Land everything immutably in a raw lake exactly as received — schema-on-read, partitioned by source + ingest time.

Crucially, load raw first and transform later (ELT), not transform-then-load (classic ETL). Keeping the untouched raw data is what makes reprocessing possible: any modeled output can be rebuilt from the raw lake when logic changes.

Get the CDC contract right, because it determines correctness downstream. Persist source offsets/LSNs and connector checkpoints so a connector can resume or replay exactly. Stamp every raw record with primary key, event time, ingest time, source version, and operation type (insert/update/delete) — and represent deletes explicitly as tombstones rather than just absence, so downstream can apply them. Watermarks here track completeness (we have everything up to time T), not merely what was processed.

Out-of-order and late events are normal. The default micro-batch path handles them by reopening the affected output partitions and MERGEing keyed rows, or routing very late corrections to the backfill path; only the lowest-latency sources need a true streaming transform. Different sources also have different cadences (CDC near-real-time; batch periodic) — the lake normalizes them into a common landing zone.

Because ELT deliberately keeps unmodified raw, the raw lake is the most sensitive part of the system, so governance must cover it from the moment data lands — not just the modeled outputs. Raw is encrypted at rest, access is least-privilege and every read is audited, and a classification step tags sensitive columns (PII/secret) as they arrive. Modeled outputs then apply masking/tokenization per class so analysts see only what their access policy allows, while retention and deletion policies (including legal hold and per-subject erasure / crypto-shred) are enforced over both raw and modeled layers.

Erasure must propagate, or ELT's retained raw will quietly defeat it. A deletion request either records a tombstone for the subject or crypto-shreds its per-subject key, then schedules rewrites of the modeled partitions that contained it (marking the superseded snapshots expired) — and the same erasure policy is recorded so that any future backfill from raw applies it too. A legal hold blocks the request until the hold is lifted. Deleting only modeled rows is not enough: retained raw would re-create the subject the next time those partitions are reprocessed.

view.caption: Source systems feed a CDC/connectors path and an event stream, both of which land raw records immutably into the landing/raw lake — which the governance service classifies, encrypts, and gates access to from the moment data lands.

options.name: ELT: land raw first, transform later (default)
options.description: Sources land untouched into a raw lake first, then warehouse/Spark transforms read that raw and write modeled tables on their own schedule.
pros: Keeps the untouched raw extract, so any modeled output can be rebuilt when logic or schema changes.
Decouples ingest cadence from transform: CDC, streams, and batch all land before modeling runs.
Transformation runs in the warehouse/Spark at scale, not in a fragile single extract script.
cons: Stores raw plus modeled copies, so storage cost roughly doubles.
Sensitive fields land in the lake before any masking, so governance must cover raw too.
Adds a landing + load + transform hop, so the simplest pipeline gets more moving parts.

view.caption: Sources flow through CDC/connectors and the event stream into the raw landing lake, kept untouched for later transformation; governance classifies, encrypts, and gates the raw lake on landing.

options.name: ETL: transform before loading
options.description: A single extract-transform-load job cleans and reshapes each source on the way in and writes only the finished modeled rows to the warehouse.
pros: Only modeled, cleaned data is stored, so warehouse storage stays smaller.
Sensitive fields can be masked/dropped before anything is persisted downstream.
Familiar batch-ETL shape that maps to a single transform-then-load job.
cons: Raw is discarded, so a transform bug or new business rule cannot be reprocessed from source.
Transform logic sits on the hot ingest path, so a schema surprise breaks the load.
Coupling extract, transform, and load in one job makes backfills and retries harder.

view.caption: A single transformation engine reshapes each source on the way in and writes only finished modeled rows to the warehouse.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why land raw data first and transform later (ELT) instead of transforming before loading (ETL)?

concepts.definition: Load raw, immutable data first; transform inside the warehouse afterward.
concepts.whyItMatters: Retaining raw enables reprocessing and decouples ingestion from evolving transform logic.
concepts.example: CDC + extracts land in the lake untouched; transforms run later over them.
concepts.group: Ingest into a Raw Lake (ELT, not ETL)

concepts.definition: Raw records carry PK, event time, ingest time, source version, and op type; deletes are explicit tombstones; offsets/LSNs are checkpointed for replay.
concepts.whyItMatters: Without it you can't dedupe, apply deletes, handle late/out-of-order events, or replay a connector — correctness breaks downstream.
concepts.example: A deleted order lands as an op=delete tombstone keyed by order_id, applied by the keyed MERGE.
concepts.group: Ingest into a Raw Lake (ELT, not ETL)

concepts.definition: Raw is encrypted with least-privilege, audited access; sensitive columns are classified on landing; modeled outputs mask/tokenize per class; retention/deletion/legal-hold are enforced over raw and modeled layers.
concepts.whyItMatters: ELT keeps unmodified raw for reprocessing, which makes the raw lake the most sensitive store — without a policy plane, retaining raw overstates how safe it is.
concepts.example: raw.orders.email lands tagged class=pii; analysts get a tokenized value; a GDPR erasure crypto-shreds the key unless a legal hold is set.
concepts.group: Ingest into a Raw Lake (ELT, not ETL)

whyNow: ELT + an immutable raw lake is the foundational decision; reprocessing, schema flexibility, and decoupling all flow from keeping raw data.

recap.before: A transform-first script loading into the operational DB.
recap.after: Heterogeneous sources (CDC + batch + stream) landed immutably in a raw lake, with offsets, op types, and tombstones captured.
recap.newRisk: Raw data isn't queryable/modeled — it must be loaded and transformed, reliably and without duplicates.

traps.trap: Honoring a deletion request by deleting only the modeled rows.
traps.why: ELT retains the subject in immutable raw, so the next backfill that reprocesses those partitions re-creates the 'erased' rows — the deletion silently regresses.
traps.instead: Crypto-shred the subject's key (or tombstone it) in raw, schedule rewrites of the affected modeled partitions, and record the erasure so future backfills apply the same policy — unless a legal hold blocks it.

steps.title: 3. Idempotent Loads and ELT Transformation
description: Load raw into typed staging tables, then transform staging into modeled analytical tables (fact/dimension marts) with SQL/Spark inside the warehouse. Process by partition (e.g. per day) so work is bounded and parallelizable.

Make loads idempotent: a load for a given partition replaces that partition's output rather than appending — so re-running a failed/late load doesn't create duplicates. A run watermark records what's been processed. Be precise about what the watermark means here: it is the output-partition-completion marker (this partition is published), distinct from the source offset/LSN the ingest layer tracks and from a freshness timestamp — those are not interchangeable.

Publish atomically so a reader never sees a half-written partition: the transform writes its output to a temporary table/location (an unpublished snapshot), runs validation (row counts, schema, freshness, quality gates), then publishes via an atomic partition swap, a warehouse transaction, or a table-format snapshot commit. Only after that commit does the watermark advance, and queries are pinned to committed snapshots — so a failed run leaves the last good partition in place and a retry simply re-publishes.

The visibility boundary is the run state: only committed snapshots are queryable. A run walks queued → loading → transforming → validating → publishing → succeeded, and the output is invisible until the publish step commits. A failed or cancelled run aborts its temp snapshot (it never becomes visible), the last committed snapshot stays in place, and dependents stay blocked until a committed snapshot exists.
This idempotent, partition-at-a-time, publish-then-advance design is what makes the pipeline reliable and retryable, and is the precondition for clean backfills later.

view.caption: The load service reads raw from the lake into staging tables, then the transformation engine models them into partitioned warehouse tables. Each transform writes to a temporary location, validates, then atomically publishes the partition before the watermark advances.

options.name: Idempotent partition overwrite (default)
options.description: Each load writes one partition (e.g. a day) to a temporary location, validates it, atomically publishes (partition swap / snapshot commit), then advances the watermark — so retries replace the partition wholesale with no duplicates and no half-written reads.
pros: Re-running a failed or late load for dt=X replaces that partition, so retries never duplicate rows.
Atomic publish + committed-snapshot reads mean queries never see a partially written partition.
A run watermark (output-completion marker) makes the pipeline resumable and safe to re-trigger.
Bounded per-partition work parallelizes cleanly and is the precondition for clean backfills.
cons: Overwriting a whole partition rewrites unchanged rows too, wasting I/O on large partitions.
Safe only when inputs are partition-aligned; late CDC events for an old partition force a full repartition rewrite (or push you to keyed MERGE / a change table).
No row-level history within a partition unless modeled explicitly.

view.caption: The load service writes staging, the transformation engine models it, and each run overwrites its target partition wholesale in the warehouse.

options.name: Append + dedup on read
options.description: Every load appends new rows to the target table, and queries deduplicate at read time using a natural key plus version column.
pros: Writes are simple appends, so loads are fast and never block on existing data.
Full append history is retained, useful for audit or late-arriving corrections.
No need to align inputs to partition boundaries before writing.
cons: Duplicates accumulate on every retry and must be de-duplicated at query time, slowing reads.
Dedup keys (natural key + version) must be perfect or stale rows leak into results.
Storage grows with every reload instead of being bounded per partition.

view.caption: The load service and transformation engine append modeled rows to the warehouse, leaving deduplication to read-time queries.

options.name: MERGE / upsert by key
options.description: A single MERGE statement keyed on the primary key inserts new rows and updates changed ones, converging the target to the latest staged state.
pros: Updates and inserts in one statement keyed by primary key, so retries converge to the correct row.
Handles slowly-changing dimensions and late updates without rewriting whole partitions.
Natural fit for warehouses with native MERGE support.
cons: MERGE against a billion-row table is far more expensive than a partition swap.
Concurrent merges can deadlock or require careful locking on the target.
Correctness hinges on a stable, unique business key for every row.

view.caption: The load service feeds staging and the transformation engine MERGEs keyed rows into the warehouse, inserting new keys and updating changed ones.

steps.decisionPrompt: A load job fails halfway and is retried. How do you avoid duplicate rows in the warehouse?

concepts.definition: Load/transform a partition such that re-running replaces (not appends) its output.
concepts.whyItMatters: Makes pipelines safe to retry and backfill without duplicates.
concepts.example: Reload dt=2026-06-01 -> overwrite that partition, not add to it.

whyNow: Idempotent partitioned processing is the reliability backbone; it's what makes retries and backfills correct.

recap.newRisk: Sources change their schemas over time — the pipeline must not break.

flows.name: Idempotent partition load + atomic publish
flows.note: A partition is loaded into staging then transformed to a temporary output; only after validation does an atomic publish commit it and the watermark advance. A re-run overwrites that partition instead of appending duplicates, and readers never see the temporary output.

messages.label: block publish; temp discarded, last good partition stays

steps.title: 4. Schema Evolution
description: Source schemas change: a column is added, renamed, or retyped. Left unhandled, the next load either breaks outright or — worse — silently corrupts data. A schema registry/catalog tracks each table's schema and version, and the pipeline validates incoming data against it.

Evolve safely: additive changes (new nullable columns) flow through automatically; breaking changes (renames, type changes) are versioned and handled deliberately (map old->new, default missing fields). Because raw is kept schema-on-read, you can reinterpret old raw data under a new schema.

The catalog also gives downstream consumers a data contract and enables lineage (what schema/version produced this table). Treat a breaking change as a contract change, not a silent one: notify downstream owners (via lineage), stage the new version behind the current one so consumers migrate, and only then cut modeled tables over — so a source rename never surprises a dashboard.

view.caption: The load service and transformation engine both validate against the schema catalog/registry, which tracks each table's versioned schema and gates evolution.

steps.decisionPrompt: A source adds a new column and renames another. How does the pipeline keep working?

concepts.definition: Versioned table schemas with rules for additive vs breaking changes.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps pipelines robust to source change and gives downstream a stable contract.
concepts.example: New nullable column auto-flows; a rename is a new schema version with mapping.

whyNow: Schema evolution is the most common real-world pipeline-breaker; handling it via a registry is the maturity signal.

recap.after: Versioned schemas; additive auto, breaking handled deliberately.
recap.newRisk: Modeled tables are huge — analytical queries over them must be fast.

traps.trap: Auto-applying every source schema change to modeled tables.
traps.why: A source rename or type change silently maps to the wrong column and corrupts downstream marts without an error.
traps.instead: Separate raw schema capture from modeled data-contract evolution: additive auto-flows, but renames/retypes/deletes are a new schema version with explicit mapping + owner approval.

traps.why: A backfill then reinterprets old raw under a newer schema and produces results that don't match what originally shipped.
traps.instead: Pin each transform to a specific schema version so reprocessing old raw is deterministic.

flows.name: Schema check + versioned evolution
flows.note: The loader validates incoming raw against the registry; additive changes auto-register a new version, breaking changes are gated, and the transform pins the version it runs against.

messages.label: block until owner approves + mapping registered
pin schema version for this run (deterministic)

steps.title: 5. Partitioning and Columnar Serving
description: Analysts query huge modeled tables. Two techniques make this fast: partition tables by a natural key (usually date) so queries prune to relevant partitions, and store data columnar so a query reads only the columns it needs.

Partition pruning + columnar scanning means a 'last 7 days, 3 columns' query touches a tiny fraction of the table. Pre-aggregate common rollups (daily summaries) for dashboards. The query/BI layer runs SQL over these modeled, partitioned tables.

Keeping those tables fast over time is an operational job, not a one-time layout choice — and it ties directly to the capacity numbers (5 TB/day landed, ~200 queries/min, ~20 long dashboards). The 5 TB/day ingest produces many small files, so a compaction job rewrites them into right-sized files and expires old snapshots. Table statistics are refreshed so the planner prunes well, clustering/sorting within a partition (e.g. by customer or region) keeps scans tight, and materialized rollups/views are refreshed on a schedule with a clear owner.

For the ~200 queries/min plus ~20 long-running dashboards, isolate workloads: dashboards and ad-hoc analysts run on separate warehouses/queues so a heavy scan can't starve interactive queries, with per-queue concurrency limits, quotas/budgets, and kill/cancel controls. Cost telemetry attributes bytes-scanned and compute back to pipeline runs, backfills, and query workloads so spend is visible and capped. Concretely this is an async query-job contract (submit, poll, cancel): a job carries a workload class/queue, the caller's principal, an access decision (allow / column-masked / denied), an estimated-vs-scanned byte count, and compute/cost attribution — so isolation, budgets, cancellation, and governance masking are an operable API, not just a layout choice.
This is the same time-bucketing/columnar idea as analytics/logging systems, applied to the warehouse's modeled tables.

view.caption: Analysts' queries from the BI layer hit the partitioned columnar warehouse, which the transformation engine writes marts into. Queries are pinned to committed snapshots, so they never see a half-written partition.

options.name: Columnar warehouse, partitioned (default)
options.description: Modeled tables are loaded into a columnar warehouse partitioned by date, so queries prune partitions and scan only the columns they reference.
pros: Columnar storage reads only the columns a query needs, so a 3-column scan skips the rest.
Partition pruning by date limits a 'last 7 days' query to a handful of partitions.
Pre-aggregated rollups make dashboard queries near-instant.
cons: Loading data into the proprietary warehouse format duplicates it from the raw lake.
Highly selective point lookups are inefficient versus a row store.
Compute is coupled to the warehouse engine and its pricing.

view.caption: The query/BI layer reads the columnar, date-partitioned warehouse directly, pruning partitions and scanning only referenced columns.

options.name: Query-in-place over the lake (lakehouse)
options.description: A query engine runs SQL directly over partitioned columnar files in the lake via an open table format, with no separate warehouse load.
pros: Queries run directly on partitioned columnar files (Parquet) in the lake — no separate load step.
Storage and compute scale independently and you avoid a second copy of the data.
Open table formats let multiple engines query the same files.
cons: Query latency over object storage is higher and less predictable than a tuned warehouse.
Needs a table format and metadata layer for partitioning, stats, and ACID semantics.
Concurrency and small-file problems can degrade interactive BI.

view.caption: The query/BI layer plans through an open table format that prunes and scans Parquet partitions in the raw lake, with no separate warehouse.

options.name: Precomputed OLAP cubes
options.description: An OLAP cube materializes common aggregations across fixed dimensions ahead of time, and dashboards read those precomputed cells instead of scanning facts.
pros: Common slice/dice aggregations are materialized ahead of time, so dashboards are sub-second.
Predictable, cheap reads for fixed reporting dimensions.
Offloads heavy aggregation off the interactive query path.
cons: Only the modeled dimensions are fast; ad-hoc or new questions fall back to a full scan.
Cube rebuilds add pipeline work and lag behind fresh data.
Combinatorial explosion of dimensions makes cubes expensive to maintain.

view.caption: The warehouse builds rollups into an OLAP cube of materialized aggregations that the query/BI layer slices and dices.

steps.decisionPrompt: A query asks for 3 columns over last week from a billion-row table. How do you avoid scanning the whole thing?

concepts.definition: Partition by date for pruning; columnar storage to scan only needed columns.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns full-table scans into pruned, narrow scans — orders of magnitude faster.
concepts.example: WHERE dt BETWEEN ... reads 7 partitions, 3 columns.

concepts.definition: Keep served tables healthy and bounded: compaction + snapshot expiry, refreshed statistics, clustering/sorting, scheduled materialized-rollup refresh, separate warehouses/queues for dashboards vs ad-hoc, and per-queue concurrency/quota/budget + kill controls.
concepts.whyItMatters: Layout alone degrades over time (small files, stale stats) and a few heavy queries can starve everyone and blow the budget — health + isolation are what keep the capacity numbers achievable and the cost bounded.
concepts.example: Dashboards run on an isolated warehouse; a nightly compaction merges small files and refreshes stats; cost telemetry attributes scan bytes to each pipeline/query workload.

whyNow: Query performance is the warehouse's reason to exist; partitioning + columnar is the standard mechanism analysts feel directly.

recap.newRisk: When transform logic or schema changes, existing modeled data is wrong — need to reprocess.

traps.trap: Over-partitioning (e.g. by hour x source x region).
traps.why: Tiny partitions create millions of small files; metadata and open-file overhead dominate and queries slow down.
traps.instead: Partition coarsely (usually by date), then cluster/sort within a partition and run periodic compaction + stats collection.

traps.trap: Letting heavy dashboard queries share the same compute as ad-hoc analysts.
traps.why: A few long-running dashboard scans starve interactive queries and blow concurrency limits.
traps.instead: Isolate workloads with separate warehouses/queues and use materialized rollups for fixed dashboards.

flows.name: Analytical query with partition pruning
flows.note: The query layer scans only the partitions and columns the query needs, not the whole table.

messages.label: SELECT 3 cols WHERE dt in last 7 days

steps.title: 6. Reprocessing and Backfills
description: Bugs in transforms, new business logic, or schema changes mean existing modeled tables become wrong or incomplete. Because raw data is retained immutably, you can rebuild any output: a backfill re-runs the load+transform over a historical date range from the raw lake.

Backfills reuse idempotent partitioned processing: reprocessing dt=2026-01-01..03-31 overwrites those partitions cleanly, no duplicates, while leaving untouched ranges alone. Each partition still goes through the same temp-write → validate → atomic publish path, so a backfill never exposes half-written history.

Backfills need admission control so historical work doesn't starve fresh-data SLAs: submit with a priority below fresh data, a concurrency cap on parallel partitions, fresh-data preemption, a dry-run cost/size estimate, owner approval for large rewrites, cancellation, and a bounded blast radius. The orchestrator runs them as bounded, parallel partition jobs in their own queue.
This reprocessability is the payoff of ELT + immutable raw + idempotent partitions: fixing history is a re-run, not data loss.

view.caption: The pipeline orchestrator re-schedules the load service and transformation engine over a historical range, replaying raw from the lake back into the warehouse.

steps.decisionPrompt: You discover a transform bug that corrupted 3 months of a table. How do you fix it?

concepts.definition: Reprocess a historical range from retained raw data via idempotent partition runs.
concepts.whyItMatters: Fixes bugs/logic changes across history without losing or duplicating data.
concepts.example: Re-run Jan-Mar partitions -> overwrite them with corrected logic.

whyNow: Backfill is the capstone that ties ELT + idempotency together; it's a top real-world requirement and a clear senior signal.

recap.after: History rebuilt by reprocessing raw, no duplicates, admission-controlled below fresh data.
recap.newRisk: Many pipelines, dependencies, and data-quality concerns must be orchestrated and monitored.

traps.trap: Running a large backfill at the same priority as fresh data.
traps.why: 90 days of reprocessing saturates the transform cluster and blows the freshness SLA for today's data.
traps.instead: Submit backfills to a separate lower-priority queue with a concurrency cap and fresh-data preemption.

traps.trap: Kicking off a multi-month backfill with no estimate or approval.
traps.why: An unbounded rewrite can cost a fortune and corrupt good partitions if the new logic is wrong.
traps.instead: Require a dry-run cost/size estimate, owner approval for large ranges, and an idempotency key so a re-submit doesn't double the work.

failureDrills.scenario: A backfill publishes incorrect history (the corrected transform was itself wrong).
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Because each partition published an atomic snapshot, roll the affected partitions back to their previous committed snapshots, pause dependent jobs so the bad data doesn't propagate, alert the owners, then re-run the backfill with a fixed transform version.
failureDrills.mitigation: Per-partition atomic snapshots + retained prior snapshots + dependency gating + a pinned transform version per re-run.

flows.name: Backfill a date range from raw
flows.note: Reprocessing reads retained raw from the lake and overwrites the affected partitions; untouched ranges are left alone.

steps.title: 7. Orchestration, Quality, and Lineage
description: Pipelines have dependencies (transform B needs table A loaded). An orchestrator runs them as DAGs: schedule loads/transforms, enforce dependencies, retry failed steps, and manage backfills — with run metadata (watermarks, state) making runs resumable and idempotent.

Data quality is first-class and wired into the publish boundary: validate freshness (did today's partition arrive?), row counts, schema conformance, and anomalies on the temporary output before it is published. Blocking checks gate the atomic publish (a failure leaves the last good snapshot in place and alerts the owning team); advisory checks warn but allow publish. Every check has an owner, alert route, and remediation/retry path. Lineage (which sources/transform version produced which snapshot) aids debugging and impact analysis, down to dataset/snapshot granularity.
Scale: ingestion, transform compute, and the warehouse scale independently; the raw lake and warehouse are partitioned; the orchestrator coordinates without being on the data path.

view.caption: The orchestrator schedules the transformation engine as a DAG while the data-quality service validates staging inputs and warehouse outputs; the catalog records lineage (which sources/transform version produced which snapshot) once a publish succeeds.

steps.decisionPrompt: Transform B depends on table A, and A's load was late. How does the system avoid running B on stale data?
whyNow: Closing on orchestration + data quality + lineage shows the platform is operable, trustworthy, and dependency-aware — the production reality.

concepts.definition: Blocking checks (freshness, row count, schema) must pass before the atomic publish, so a failure leaves the last good snapshot in place and alerts the owner; advisory checks (e.g. anomaly) warn but still allow publish.
concepts.whyItMatters: Wiring quality into the publish boundary is what keeps a bad partition from ever becoming queryable or propagating to dependents — without it, validation is just an after-the-fact alarm.
concepts.example: A row-count check fails -> publish is blocked, dependents stay blocked, owner is paged; an anomaly check warns -> publish proceeds with a flagged snapshot.

concepts.definition: Record provenance per run/snapshot — which source offsets/LSNs and transform version produced which output snapshot — not merely table-to-table edges.
concepts.whyItMatters: Run/snapshot-level lineage is what makes impact analysis and audit precise: you can answer 'which transform version and inputs produced this exact bad partition?' instead of just 'these tables are related'.
concepts.example: fct_orders snap_77 -> produced by run r_9, transformVersion v42, from raw.orders up to lsn:48211.

recap.newRisk: Pipeline failures and late data — bounded by orchestration retries, quality gates, and watermarks.

bottlenecks.issue: A late upstream load stalls dependents.
bottlenecks.mitigation: DAG dependencies + freshness checks gate downstream runs.
Bounded parallel partition jobs; prioritize fresh data over backfill.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The orchestrator retries; idempotent partition processing means the re-run overwrites cleanly with no duplicates.
failureDrills.mitigation: Idempotent partitions + run watermarks + orchestrator retry.

traps.trap: Treating lineage as table-level metadata only.
traps.why: Table-to-table edges can't answer 'which inputs and transform version produced this exact bad partition?', so impact analysis and audit stay coarse and a backfill can't be reasoned about precisely.
traps.instead: Record lineage per run/snapshot: source offsets/LSNs consumed, the pinned transform version, and the output snapshot produced.

traps.trap: Running advisory quality checks as if they were blocking (or vice versa).
traps.why: Blocking on a noisy anomaly check stalls fresh data on false positives; treating a freshness/row-count check as advisory lets a bad partition publish and propagate to dependents.
traps.instead: Classify each check explicitly: correctness checks (freshness, row count, schema) block the publish; observational checks (anomaly/drift) warn but allow it, each with an owner and alert route.

flows.name: Quality-gated publish with lineage (success + blocking failure)
flows.note: The orchestrator runs a DAG node only once its dependencies are fresh, transforms to a temp snapshot, then quality gates the atomic publish. On pass: commit the snapshot, record lineage, advance the watermark, and unblock dependents. On a blocking failure: leave the last good snapshot visible, record the failed check, alert the owner, and wait for retry/remediation.

messages.label: block publish; record failed check; alert owner
last good snapshot stays; await retry / remediation

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Data Warehouse / ETL Platform
finalDesign.description: Heterogeneous sources (CDC, streams, batch/API) land immutably in a raw lake (ELT), with raw records carrying primary key, event time, ingest time, source version, and operation type, and connectors checkpointing source offsets/LSNs. A load service idempotently loads partitions into staging; a transform engine models them into partitioned, columnar warehouse tables (fact/dim) validated against a schema registry that handles evolution. The publish boundary is explicit: transforms write to a temporary/unpublished snapshot, quality checks validate it, an atomic partition swap / snapshot commit makes it visible, and only then does the output watermark advance — queries are pinned to committed snapshots so they never read a half-written partition. Analysts query the modeled tables with partition pruning + columnar scans, with table health (compaction, statistics, clustering) and workload isolation keeping serving fast and costs bounded. Retained raw data makes any output reprocessable via idempotent partition backfills, admission-controlled below fresh-data priority. A pipeline orchestrator runs the load/transform DAGs with dependencies, retries, watermarks, and backfills; data-quality checks gate downstream trust (blocking vs advisory) and lineage tracks provenance. Governance is a first-class platform responsibility: raw is encrypted with least-privilege, audited access, sensitive columns are classified and masked/tokenized in modeled outputs, and retention/deletion/legal-hold policies are enforced. Ingestion, transform compute, and the warehouse scale independently.

view.caption: Sources land via CDC/connectors and the event stream into the raw lake; the load service stages, the transformation engine models into the columnar warehouse (validated by the schema catalog), all orchestrated and quality-checked, with governance classifying/masking/auditing access and the BI layer querying.

functional.how: CDC + batch + stream landed in a raw lake.

nonFunctional.how: Partition-replace keyed by idempotency key: write a temp snapshot, validate, atomic publish, then advance the output watermark. Readers pin to committed snapshots, so a retry re-publishes with no duplicates and no half-written reads.
CDC near-real-time; batch scheduled by orchestrator.
Encrypted raw, classified sensitive columns, least-privilege + audited access, masking/tokenization on modeled outputs, and enforced retention/deletion/legal-hold.

tradeoff: Object storage is the durable replay source for raw CDC, stream, and batch extracts; it is cheap, append-friendly, and separates storage from transform compute.
Self-hosted object stores keep data under your operational control, but you own durability, replication, lifecycle policy, encryption, and capacity planning.
Managed object storage gives lifecycle tiers, encryption, audit hooks, and near-unlimited scale, but backfill and query costs must be governed.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A governed object store with lifecycle rules and retention policies removes most hand-built archival, replay-file, and raw-retention plumbing.

tradeoff: Self-hosted CDC gives precise control over offsets, tombstones, schemas, and source-specific failure handling, but connector operations become part of the platform.
Managed ingestion shortens time-to-value for common databases and SaaS sources, but may hide replay semantics or charge heavily at high row volume.
For correctness, choose tools that expose stable offsets/LSNs and let the raw lake persist source version, operation type, and ingest metadata.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A connector platform with checkpointing, retries, and dead-letter handling reduces custom per-source extract scripts.

tradeoff: Kafka-compatible logs fit ordered CDC/event replay and multiple downstream consumers, but require partition and retention management.
Cloud streams remove broker operations and integrate with provider warehouses and object stores, but portability and throughput controls vary.
A stream is most valuable for low-latency sources; bulk extracts still belong in the raw lake with the same metadata contract.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed stream with retention, consumer lag metrics, and capture-to-object-storage support removes much custom stream archiving code.

tradeoff: Open table formats provide snapshot isolation, schema evolution, atomic publish, and time travel over object storage.
They add metadata maintenance: compaction, snapshot expiry, manifest cleanup, and catalog availability.
Warehouse-native tables are simpler when one engine owns the workload; lakehouse formats are stronger when many engines must read the same data.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Snapshot commits replace fragile hand-written atomic partition swap logic for many transform outputs.

tradeoff: SQL-first modeling is easiest for warehouse-native ELT, reviewable transforms, tests, and analyst ownership.
Spark handles large batch reshapes and file maintenance; Flink fits the subset of sources that need continuous streaming transforms.
Managed compute removes cluster operations but can make local reproducibility and cross-cloud portability harder.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A mature transform framework with tests and lineage reduces bespoke SQL runner code and ad-hoc dependency tracking.

tradeoff: Managed warehouses give elastic query serving, workload isolation, materialized views, and lower operational load at a provider-specific cost model.
Self-hosted OLAP engines can be cheaper for steady workloads or specialized latency goals, but require tuning, upgrades, and concurrency protection.
Serverless query engines are useful over raw/lakehouse tables, but hot dashboards often need curated marts, rollups, or dedicated compute.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A warehouse with native materialized views and workload queues can remove separate OLAP cube products for many dashboard workloads.

technologyChoices.concern: BI / semantic layer and dashboard serving
tradeoff: A semantic layer centralizes metric definitions and keeps dashboards from embedding inconsistent business logic.
Self-hosted BI gives control and lower fixed software cost, but you operate auth, extracts, scheduling, and dashboard concurrency.
Managed BI integrates with provider IAM and warehouses, but licensing and egress can dominate at broad company adoption.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A shared semantic model reduces one-off dashboard SQL and repeated metric reconciliation.

tradeoff: Airflow-style schedulers have broad ecosystems and familiar DAG semantics, but can become code-heavy for asset dependencies and backfills.
Asset-aware orchestrators model datasets, freshness, and lineage more directly, which fits this interview's output snapshot contract.
Managed orchestrators remove scheduler operations but can constrain custom retry, cancellation, and admission-control behavior.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: An orchestrator with retries, dependency gating, run history, and backfill primitives removes most custom cron and run-state tables.

tradeoff: A registry makes source schemas versioned and reviewable, so additive changes can flow while breaking changes require owner approval.
Catalog products add discovery and governance metadata, but they only protect downstream jobs if loaders and transforms enforce compatibility.
Event schemas and table schemas should share policy even if they live in different products.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Schema compatibility checks remove brittle transform assumptions that always read the latest source shape.

tradeoff: Quality gates catch row-count, freshness, schema, and anomaly failures before a bad snapshot becomes queryable.
Lineage explains which source offsets, transform version, and run produced an output snapshot, but requires every task to emit metadata consistently.
Provider observability is quick to wire into logs and alerts; open lineage tools are more portable across engines and clouds.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Standard quality checks and lineage events reduce bespoke audit spreadsheets and manual run investigations.

tradeoff: Governance must start at raw landing because ELT intentionally stores sensitive source data before masking.
Central policy products simplify column/row grants, masking, audit, and legal hold, but every engine must be integrated with the policy plane.
Self-managed policy and key systems are portable, but compliance evidence and least-privilege operations become your burden.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A policy plane with catalog tags, encryption keys, audit logs, and lifecycle enforcement removes scattered per-table access scripts.

say: Confirm many sources -> raw lake -> modeled queryable warehouse.
Pin idempotency, reprocessability, and query performance.
Note schema evolution and per-dataset freshness.
ELT: land raw immutably, transform in-warehouse.
Tradeoffs: ELT vs ETL, freshness vs cost, backfill priority.
Mention streaming-first (kappa) and data contracts as extensions.

expectations: Ingests and transforms into queryable tables.
May miss idempotency, schema evolution, and backfills.
Reasons about reprocessability and idempotency precisely.
Discusses CDC vs batch, backfill priority, and cost tradeoffs deeply.

followUps: How do you handle a breaking source schema change (rename/retype) without downtime?
How do you keep backfills from starving fresh-data pipelines?
When would you go streaming-first (kappa) instead of batch ELT?
How do you guarantee a query never reads a half-written partition?
How would you enforce data contracts/quality before data reaches analysts?
